title: Prepare an input clip
summary: Use this rail to set the stages that feed the full detector -> tracking -> calibration -> overlay path.
body: This workspace is for short, iterative setup on one broadcast clip at a time. The goal is to make the pipeline legible before you trust longer runs or downstream summaries.

A bad choice here usually fails downstream in a recognizable order: weak detection poisons tracking, weak tracking makes team separation noisy, and poor field registration makes the minimap or projection unreliable even if the boxes look reasonable.

title: Analysis pipeline
summary: Selects the detection and field-registration architecture that drives the entire analysis run.
body: Classic uses the soccana detector for player/ball detection plus soccana_keypoint for field registration. These are separate models with independent weights and confidence thresholds.

SoccerMaster is a unified architecture that handles detection and field-line regression in a single forward pass. When selected, the separate detector-weights and keypoint-model controls are hidden because the unified model replaces both.

sn-gamestate is different again: it is an external TrackLab-based Game State Reconstruction baseline from SoccerNet. In this app it behaves as an external pipeline option rather than a local detector checkpoint, so live preview is unavailable and the native detector/tracker sliders do not control it.

title: Detector weights
summary: The detector decides which players, referees, and ball candidates exist at all. Every downstream stage depends on it.
body: In this repo, `soccana` is the football-specific detector path. It is preferred because the app is tuned around football broadcast footage rather than generic COCO-style scenes.

If the detector misses small distant players, confuses referees, or hallucinates crowd and board boxes, the tracker, team clustering, and ball coverage all inherit that damage. Detector swaps are useful only when they improve the overlay, not because they sound more advanced.

title: Player tracker
summary: The tracker turns frame-local detections into identities that survive motion, occlusion, and re-entry.
body: `bytetrack` is the fast baseline. It follows detections frame to frame, but it does not try to reconnect long identity gaps once a player disappears or the scene changes too aggressively.

`hybrid_reid` adds sparse appearance embeddings and a stitch pass. That gives you two identity layers: raw online IDs from the live tracker and canonical stitched IDs after the merge pass. Raw-vs-stitched counts are useful because they tell you whether the post-pass is cleaning up tracker churn or inventing over-confident merges.
Use the overlay and the raw-vs-stitched metrics together. A lower stitched ID count is only good if the video still looks coherent when players cross, crowd, or leave and re-enter the frame.

links.label: Long-term player tracking with graph hierarchies
Team-Aware Football Player Tracking with SAM

title: Include ball tracking
summary: Ball tracking is a supporting signal for match context, not the primary success condition for the pipeline.
body: The ball is small, fast, heavily occluded, and often ambiguous against socks, feet, or ad boards. Good player coverage can coexist with weak ball continuity.
Use ball output to judge whether a clip reads like real play and to sanity-check events, but do not let a weak ball trace distract you from fixing detection, tracking, or calibration first.

title: Confidence and IOU thresholds
summary: These values control how aggressively the runtime accepts detections and how permissive box matching is.
body: Higher confidence usually removes more false positives but can also erase small distant players and the ball. Lower confidence recovers recall, but it can flood the tracker with garbage detections that create churn.

IOU settings affect how readily overlapping boxes are treated as the same object. Threshold tuning is meaningful only after you inspect the overlay; the right values depend on the clip scale, crowding, and camera motion.

title: Automatic field registration
summary: Field calibration refreshes from pitch keypoints and quality gates. It can fail even when player boxes look healthy.
body: This pipeline refreshes pitch geometry from field keypoints, then accepts or rejects each candidate homography based on visibility and geometric quality. A run can have good detections and still lose minimap quality if calibration refreshes keep failing.

The review metrics around calibration success, stale recovery, gate rejects, and projection stages are there to show whether the minimap is using fresh geometry, coasting on stale state, or being blocked entirely.

Treat field registration as a live runtime component, not a cosmetic post-process. If calibration is weak, projected player positions and ball locations should be considered suspect even when image-space boxes look stable.

title: SoccerNet browsing and download
summary: SoccerNet is the benchmark and dataset surface behind much of the football CV ecosystem this app borrows from.
body: This section is not just a file picker. It is the bridge between the local app and current football-specific benchmark tasks such as action spotting, calibration, tracking, and game-state reconstruction.

Use it when you want current benchmark footage and labels without manually copying paths every session. `Labels-v2.json` remains important because several higher-level experiments and event-aligned analyses expect that structure.

title: Scan a local dataset folder
summary: Folder scan is a convenience layer for turning a local dataset tree into clickable clip and label choices.
body: Use this when your footage already lives on disk and you want the app to surface candidate videos and annotation files without manually retyping full paths.
This is operational help, not a model or benchmark concept, so there are no external references here.

title: Input Clip
summary: This panel is the raw source video. It is where you verify what the model is seeing before trusting any overlay.
body: Use the raw clip to compare against live preview and saved overlay outputs. If the source itself is confusing or cropped badly, every downstream artifact will be harder to interpret.
When something looks wrong in review, come back here first and confirm that the failure is not caused by the clip segment, framing, or source mismatch.

title: Live Inference Preview
summary: Live preview is the fast visual debugging surface for runtime behavior before you commit to a full saved run.
body: Use this mode to catch detector misses, ID churn, and calibration dropouts quickly. It is deliberately lighter than a full analysis run so you can iterate before waiting for saved outputs.
If the preview already looks weak, do not expect the saved review to rescue it. Fix the earliest broken stage first, then rerun.

title: Saved Run Review
summary: Review mode is where you judge the saved overlay, diagnostics, and exported metrics for one completed run.
body: The review surface should answer two questions quickly: did this run actually work, and which pipeline stage needs attention first if it did not.
Use the overlay as the primary artifact. Metrics and diagnostics are there to explain what the video is already showing, not to replace it with abstract counters.

title: Training Studio
summary: Training Studio scans a local detector dataset, runs a fine-tune, then lets you review and activate the resulting checkpoint for analysis.
body: This surface is detector-only on purpose. It keeps dataset validation, local fine-tuning, job review, and registry activation in one loop.
Use it when the stock `soccana` checkpoint does not fit your camera angle or footage domain. A successful run should improve the downstream analysis overlay, not just create a new file.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Active detector
summary: This is the checkpoint the analysis workspace will use whenever the detector selector stays on `soccana`.
body: A finished job is not live until its registry entry is activated. A checkpoint can exist on disk and still have no effect on analysis.
If analysis behavior changes unexpectedly, check this first. The visible detector selector can still say `soccana` while a custom checkpoint is active underneath it.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Dataset intake
summary: Dataset intake validates a local YOLO detection dataset before the studio enables fine-tuning.
body: The scan checks split structure, label files, class names, and the football class contract the analysis pipeline expects.
If intake is wrong, fix the dataset first. Hyperparameter tuning will not rescue missing splits or bad player, ball, or referee IDs.

title: Dataset path
summary: Point this at the detector dataset root, not a single image folder or an output directory.
body: The studio expects a YOLO-style dataset layout it can scan and turn into a run-local manifest. If the root is wrong, the scan fails before training starts.
The source dataset stays in place. The worker writes its own generated runtime YAML inside the training run folder.

title: Scan result
summary: The scan result is a readiness report for the actual training contract, not just a file-system summary.
body: This panel explains whether the dataset is coherent enough to start detector fine-tuning safely. It should make clear what the studio found, what it had to infer, and what would make the resulting metrics less trustworthy.

A green result means the studio believes the split structure, label files, and class mapping are coherent enough to proceed. A warning means training may still work, but the operator should understand the compromise. A blocking result means the worker should not start.
A blocking result can also mean the path is a different dataset format entirely, such as a FiftyOne export with JSON annotations instead of a YOLO detector dataset with per-image label text files.
Use this panel to decide whether to fix data quality first. If the scan result looks weak, the right answer is usually in dataset structure or labels, not in hyperparameter tuning.

title: Validation strategy
summary: This tells you exactly where validation images come from for the run, which determines how honest the reported metrics can be.
body: `existing_split` means the dataset already had validation images and the studio keeps them. `generate_from_train` means the backend deterministically peels a validation list out of the discovered train images. `reuse_train_single_image` means the same image is used for both train and val because there is only one sample.

Those strategies are not equivalent. Compare metrics across runs only after checking this field, because a good score on a recycled or improvised validation slice should carry less weight than the same score on a real split.

title: Class mapping
summary: This is the semantic bridge from dataset class IDs to the football roles the workbench actually uses downstream.
body: The scanner looks for label hints that mean player or goalkeeper, ball, and referee. Goalkeeper is intentionally folded into player-side identity handling because the analysis pipeline needs a usable football person class more than it needs a separate keeper workflow here.
If these IDs are wrong, the checkpoint can train successfully and still be operationally broken. A ball class mapped to the wrong ID or a missing player mapping poisons analysis even if mAP goes up.

title: Training family
summary: Training family keeps the studio scoped to the detector workflow that the app supports today.
body: Right now the active family is detector fine-tuning only. That keeps the form, jobs view, and registry aligned with the current backend.
If more model families arrive later, they should be added as distinct workflows instead of overloading this one.

title: Detector fine-tuning
summary: Detector fine-tuning is the controlled workflow that turns a validated football detector dataset into a candidate checkpoint plus enough evidence to decide whether it belongs in analysis.
body: This form is where dataset validation, runtime defaults, and promotion rules become a real training run. It should feel like the operator-facing contract for how this repo adapts detectors, not like a thin shell-command wrapper.

A good fine-tune run should leave behind more than weights. It should leave a readable story: what dataset was used, what runtime trained it, what the metrics looked like, and why the resulting checkpoint should or should not become active.
That is the standard to hold this workflow to. If the run cannot teach the operator what happened, it is not yet a high-quality training surface.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Base weights
summary: This chooses the checkpoint the fine-tune starts from. In this studio, that starting point is the football prior for players, ball, and referees.
body: `soccana` is not just a default label. It already knows football broadcast object scale and class structure, so the run starts from a detector that has seen the kinds of players, keepers, referees, and ball examples this workbench cares about.

Changing this field changes how much relearning the model must do before it becomes useful. A football-pretrained base usually recovers tiny-ball and distant-player behavior much faster than a generic checkpoint would.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Run name
summary: This is the human label that follows the run through Jobs and Registry. It is how you remember what changed without opening config files.
body: The backend still gives each run a timestamped `run_id`, but this field becomes the readable name shown on the job card and the registry entry. It is the operator-facing identity of the experiment.

Use it to encode the camera domain, dataset revision, or experiment intent, such as `wide-cam-soccernet-v2` or `broadcast-low-angle-ball-recall`. Good names make later promotion decisions much easier to audit.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Training hyperparameters
summary: These controls shape how long the run trains, how much fits per step, and how aggressively the checkpoint adapts.
body: Epochs, image size, batch, workers, patience, and freeze trade runtime cost against adaptation strength and stability.
Start with a smaller smoke run when you are validating a new dataset. It is cheaper to catch bad labels or class mapping early.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Training device
summary: This is the requested hardware path for training. `auto` is only a request; the worker resolves the actual device when the run starts.
body: In the current backend, `auto` resolves in a simple order: MPS first if available, then CUDA, then CPU. That makes this field a practical runtime choice, not just a convenience toggle.

The device changes throughput, memory ceiling, and failure mode. CPU is slower but conservative. MPS and CUDA are faster when healthy, but they are also where memory and backend-specific instability show up first.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Runtime behavior
summary: This explains what the worker writes while the run is active and why those files matter after training ends.
body: Each run gets its own folder under `backend/training_runs/<run_id>/`. The worker writes a generated `dataset_runtime.yaml`, split text files, `progress.json`, `train.log`, Ultralytics output under `yolo_output/train/`, a copied `weights/best.pt`, a top-level `summary.json`, and a compact `training_provenance.json`.

When you activate a detector, the studio also copies the checkpoint into `backend/models/promoted/custom_<run_id>/` and writes matching provenance there. That gives Registry a stable promotion target instead of relying only on the transient run folder.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Training jobs
summary: The Jobs tab is the run-by-run audit surface for detector training, and it should teach what happened, what it cost, and whether the checkpoint is activation-worthy.
body: Read a job card top to bottom. The metadata tells you what was trained and where. The progress and epoch state tell you how far the run got. The metrics tell you what the validation split thinks happened. The artifacts and logs tell you whether that story is trustworthy.

A finished job is not automatically a good job. If the dataset contract was weak, if the resolved device fell back in an unexpected way, or if the metrics look good but the overlay later looks worse, the right move is to reject or rerun the checkpoint instead of promoting it blindly.
Use the Jobs tab to compare evidence, not just completion state. The goal is to understand whether this run is a candidate detector for the product, not simply whether the worker exited successfully.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: AI training review
summary: This is the run-level narrative that turns config, logs, metrics, dataset contract, and artifacts into one activation-focused explanation.
body: The goal of this review is not to replace raw logs or metrics. It is to connect them into a clear answer to two questions: what actually happened in this run, and should this checkpoint move closer to activation for analysis.

A strong review should separate runtime failures from weak validation, weak validation from dataset-contract issues, and dataset-contract issues from artifact-readiness or promotion problems. Those are different problems and they need different fixes.
Read the summary line first, then the five collapsible sections. The sections are intentionally stable categories so you can compare runs without guessing which evidence bucket matters most.

links.label: Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Run outcome
summary: This section says where the run actually ended and whether the result is a model-quality signal or a runtime failure signal.
body: A failed run at `0 / 50` epochs is not a weak detector result. It is usually a worker, device, callback, or training-loop execution problem. That difference matters because the right fix is in implementation or runtime configuration, not in more hyperparameter tweaking.
A completed run can still be a poor activation candidate, but it at least produced real optimization evidence. This section is there to stop those two situations from being blended together.

title: Dataset contract
summary: This section explains whether the run started from a trustworthy football detector dataset contract or from warning-heavy fallback logic.
body: Training Studio does more than count files. It checks class names, football role mapping, split structure, missing labels, and how validation images are being sourced.

A run can complete and still be operationally suspect if the dataset YAML names are weak, player or ball IDs are wrong, or the validation split had to be improvised. This section keeps that contract visible so you do not over-trust clean-looking metrics.

title: Training dynamics
summary: This section is about whether the loss and optimizer traces reflect real learning or only a partial worker startup before failure.
body: Sparse curve samples can mean very different things. On a healthy run, they are a coarse story of how the optimizer behaved. On a failed run, they may only prove that callback hooks fired before the loop stabilized.

Use this section to avoid reading too much into noisy or truncated curves. If epoch one never completed, the right interpretation is often `no valid learning signal yet`, not `interesting early overfit`.

links.label: Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Validation signal
summary: This section explains how believable the reported detector metrics are for this specific run.
body: Detector metrics only mean what the validation contract allows them to mean. A real validation split carries more weight than a generated fallback, and missing `results.csv` metrics after a crash do not tell you anything about detector quality at all.
Use this section to decide whether the numbers are evidence for promotion, evidence for caution, or simply unavailable because the worker never produced them.

links.label: Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Artifact readiness
summary: This section is the bridge from a finished run folder to a checkpoint that the product can safely consider for activation.
body: A detector is not activation-ready just because training wrote a file. This section ties together checkpoint existence, summary state, provenance, and whether the run should move forward or stay blocked.

It matters because Training Studio is a product surface, not just a folder browser. The operator needs to know whether the run produced durable evidence, a promotable checkpoint, or only a useful failure record.

title: Training analysis prompt context
summary: This is the exact evidence bundle that was sent to the model when the AI training review was generated.
body: The prompt context is there for auditability. It shows the important log chunks, recent log tail, code excerpts, and budget metadata used to build the AI review.

Use it when the review seems too vague or surprisingly specific. It tells you whether the model had enough evidence, whether the right log block made it into context, and which implementation files were quoted for the diagnosis.

title: Run artifacts
summary: Run artifacts are the files that let you audit or reuse a training job after the process exits.
body: Different artifact rows answer different questions. A generated dataset manifest tells you what `data=` contract the worker really trained on. Curves, CSVs, and plots show how optimization moved. Logs explain runtime decisions and failures. Checkpoints are the files you can actually promote.

The provenance artifact matters because it ties the dataset path, run-local manifest, promoted checkpoint, and DVC-tracked or non-tracked state into one compact record. If a run is worth keeping, the artifact set should be enough to reconstruct what happened without guessing from folder names alone.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Detector registry
summary: The registry is the promotion layer that decides which detector the analysis workspace actually trusts.
body: A completed run becomes a registry candidate, not an automatic default. The registry is where the operator compares lineage, runtime, metrics, class compatibility, promotion path, and artifact trail before changing the detector that analysis will use.

This matters because activation is a product decision, not just a training outcome. A detector can finish training cleanly and still be the wrong default if its metrics are misleading or its football behavior regresses in the overlay.

Read the registry as a shortlist of candidates plus one active choice. The quality bar is not just whether the checkpoint exists, but whether it is the right checkpoint to push into day-to-day analysis and whether its provenance is durable enough to trust later.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Runtime profile
summary: This is the host-level context behind the training recommendations the studio is making on this machine.
body: The ribbon is describing real runtime facts: backend stack, version, preferred device, and which acceleration paths are actually available right now. On one machine that may mean a Mac-first MPS flow; on another it may mean CUDA or CPU only.
Use it to calibrate expectations before you start a run. The same form behaves differently on an MPS laptop, a CUDA box, and a CPU-only host.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Detector training metrics
summary: These are the validation-split detector metrics surfaced from the training backend. They matter only if you read them in football workflow terms.
body: Precision tells you how often predicted football objects are actually right. Recall tells you how many real players, referees, and ball instances the model is catching. `mAP50` is the easier overlap score, while `mAP50-95` is stricter about box quality.

In this product, a higher number is not automatically a better detector. Extra recall is bad if it floods tracking with junk boxes, and a good `mAP50` can still hide sloppy boxes that hurt player association and ball stability.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Epochs
summary: This sets the maximum number of full passes over the generated training split before the run finishes or early stopping cuts it short.
body: More epochs give the checkpoint more time to adapt, but they also give a weak dataset more time to overfit. In this workflow, a short smoke run is often the fastest way to prove that labels, class mapping, and runtime are sane before spending a longer budget.
Read epochs as a cap on training effort, not as a quality guarantee. If the scan contract or validation strategy is weak, raising epochs usually magnifies a bad setup instead of rescuing it.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Image size
summary: This is the training resolution. It directly affects how much detail the detector keeps for far-side players and the tiny ball.
body: Football datasets punish low resolution because many important objects are small: wide-angle players near the touchline, the ball in flight, and crowded box play. A larger `imgsz` preserves more of that structure.
The cost is immediate: higher memory use, slower steps, and usually a smaller sustainable batch size. In this studio, `imgsz` and `batch` should be tuned together, not independently.

title: Batch
summary: This controls how many images the trainer processes per optimization step after the runtime dataset manifest has been built.
body: Batch size is usually the first setting that collides with local hardware limits. Raising it can improve throughput and smooth out updates, but only if the chosen device can actually hold that many images at the selected `imgsz`.
If runs start failing on MPS or CUDA, batch is one of the first places to look. If `imgsz` goes up, the workable batch size usually comes down.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Workers
summary: This sets how many host-side data loader workers feed images and labels into the trainer.
body: Workers do not change detector semantics. They change how hard the CPU and storage subsystem work to keep the training device busy.

On a fast local SSD, a few workers can keep MPS or CUDA fed smoothly. On a constrained laptop or slow disk, too many workers can create extra contention and make the whole machine feel worse without speeding training up.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Patience
summary: This is the early-stopping tolerance: how many non-improving validation epochs the run will accept before stopping.
body: Patience only means something relative to the validation strategy. With a real validation split, it helps stop flat or regressing runs before they waste more local compute.

With fallback validation, especially `generate_from_train` or `reuse_train_single_image`, the signal is weaker. In that case, patience can still stop the run, but the decision is based on less trustworthy evidence.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Freeze
summary: This decides how much of the pretrained detector stays locked instead of adapting to your dataset.
body: Higher freeze values preserve more of `soccana`'s football behavior and reduce drift. Lower values let the run rewrite more of the model so it can adapt harder to a new camera angle or annotation style.

That trade-off matters in football. Unfreezing too aggressively on a small or noisy dataset can damage useful pretrained behavior such as ball sensitivity or class separation, even if training appears to proceed normally.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Cache images
summary: This changes how the worker reads data, not what the model learns. It trades extra local memory or disk pressure for less repeated file I/O.
body: When caching helps, the trainer spends less time waiting on image reads and more time actually stepping the model. That can make local iteration smoother, especially on slower storage.
When caching hurts, it simply moves the bottleneck into memory pressure or disk churn. It does not fix bad labels, weak class mapping, or a poor validation split.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Dataset YAML
summary: This is the source dataset contract the scanner found. The worker later turns it into a run-local `dataset_runtime.yaml` that training actually consumes.
body: The scan uses the source YAML to discover split references and class names. If that file is missing, unparseable, or semantically wrong, the studio cannot safely map the trained detector back into analysis.

If the path points at a different export format, such as a FiftyOne dataset dump with JSON annotations, this field stays missing because there is no compatible detector-training YAML contract to trust.

The generated runtime manifest is only as good as the contract it starts from. If the source YAML points at the wrong splits or names classes badly, the run can still produce artifacts while learning the wrong thing.

title: Class source
summary: This is the provenance of the class names the studio is using to interpret the dataset.
body: The value is not decorative metadata. It tells you whether class names came from a real YAML path, whether that YAML existed but could not be parsed, or whether the source is missing entirely.

That matters because this product does not just need any class list. It needs names that can be mapped back into football roles the runtime understands, especially player or goalkeeper, ball, and referee.

title: Backend runtime
summary: This identifies the training implementation and version that actually produced the checkpoint you are looking at.
body: Today that means the local Ultralytics YOLO stack and its installed version, not an abstract idea of 'YOLO training'. The backend determines callback behavior, metric extraction, artifact layout, and what the logs mean.
If two runs come from materially different backend versions, compare them more cautiously. Even when the metric names look the same, the runtime that produced them is part of the evidence.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Resolved device
summary: This is the hardware path the worker actually used after resolving the requested device value.
body: For runs started with `auto`, this is the authoritative answer. In the current worker, the resolution order is MPS, then CUDA, then CPU.
Trust this field over the form input when you explain speed, memory behavior, or instability. It is execution provenance, not operator intent.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Epoch progress
summary: This shows how far the run got through its planned schedule as reported by `progress.json`.
body: It is more than a progress bar. It tells you whether the checkpoint reached enough training maturity to judge, or whether the run ended too early to mean much.
Use it with status and logs. `7 / 50` can mean a healthy early-stop decision, a manual stop, or a failure that happened before the run was ready for promotion.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: mAP50 and related detector metrics
summary: This is the forgiving detector quality score in the studio: predictions count as correct at a 0.50 IoU threshold on the validation split.
body: `mAP50` is useful because it rises early when the model starts finding the right football objects at all. It is often the quickest sanity signal that the run is not completely lost.

It is also easy to over-trust. A detector can score better at `mAP50` while still drawing loose boxes that are bad for tracking and ball localization, so always read it beside `mAP50-95`, precision, recall, and an actual overlay check.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Checkpoint
summary: This is the concrete model file the product can activate, usually the copied `weights/best.pt` for the run.
body: Once a run is promoted, this exact path is what analysis and live preview will load when the active detector points at the custom checkpoint. It is the operational artifact, not just a breadcrumb to the training folder.
A checkpoint path by itself does not prove the run was good. Its value comes from the summary, class mapping, device provenance, and overlay behavior that justify trusting that file.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Run summary artifact
summary: This is the machine-readable record of the training run that the studio reopens later instead of scraping raw logs.
body: `summary.json` ties together config, dataset scan snapshot, generated dataset manifest, validation strategy, resolved device, metrics, curves, artifact paths, and checkpoint outcome.

The summary is the broad run contract. `training_provenance.json` is the narrower lineage contract for durable artifacts. Together they let Jobs and Registry stay product-like without scraping logs or inferring promotion history from paths.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Durable training artifacts
summary: Durable artifacts are the files that should still make sense after the original training process and local shell session are long gone.
body: A local run folder is useful, but activation is where the artifact needs to become trustworthy for the product. In this studio, that means copying the active checkpoint into `backend/models/promoted/custom_<run_id>/` and writing a compact provenance file beside it.

DVC is the optional durability layer around that. When a dataset folder or promoted checkpoint has a `.dvc` pointer, the studio can show whether the artifact is just a local path or a versioned asset you can restore and compare later.

title: DVC runtime status
summary: This tells you whether the repository is configured for DVC and whether the local CLI is available to restore or inspect tracked artifacts.
body: A `ready` state means both sides exist: the repo has `.dvc/` metadata and the local machine has a usable DVC binary. `repo configured` means the repo is prepared, but the local CLI is missing. `CLI only` means DVC exists on the machine, but this repo has not been initialized for it.

This matters because a missing CLI changes what the studio can guarantee. The run can still train, but the post-checkout restore hook and any DVC-backed artifact recovery will not actually execute on that machine.

title: Training provenance
summary: Training provenance is the compact lineage record that says which dataset, manifest, checkpoint, and promotion action produced the detector you are looking at.
body: The provenance file is intentionally narrower than the full summary. It keeps the exact dataset path, generated runtime manifest, checkpoint path, activation metadata, and DVC-tracked or non-tracked state together so the operator can audit lineage quickly.

Use it to answer the uncomfortable but important questions: was this checkpoint promoted from the run I think it was, is the dataset path the expected one, and are these assets durable or just local leftovers on one machine.

title: Benchmark clip
summary: Every candidate runs the exact same clip so the leaderboard compares models instead of footage differences.
body: A benchmark only means anything when the input stays fixed. Change the clip and you change the camera angle, crowding, pitch visibility, and ball behavior all at once.
This surface locks one clip for the whole run. Every baseline or detector candidate sees the same frames, which is what makes the side-by-side review readable.

title: Shared runtime defaults
summary: The benchmark clip stays fixed, and native tracker / threshold defaults stay fixed where those controls apply.
body: Classic detector checkpoints are compared under one shared native runtime profile so tracker mode, threshold tuning, and ball settings do not drift between runs.

Some baseline candidates override the pipeline internally. SoccerMaster uses its unified detection-plus-calibration stack, and sn-gamestate runs its external TrackLab pipeline, while still sharing the same cached clip for side-by-side review.

title: Benchmark candidate
summary: A candidate can be a core pipeline baseline or a detector checkpoint evaluated against the same cached clip.
body: The core comparison set is classic / soccana, SoccerMaster, and sn-gamestate. You can also add local registry detectors or imported checkpoints to compare them against that baseline trio.
A candidate is not automatically promoted into analysis. Benchmark Lab runs it in isolation first so you can inspect overlay, diagnostics, logs, and setup readiness before changing defaults.

title: Proxy comparison score
summary: This is a transparent weighted proxy across stability, calibration, coverage, and throughput, not a labeled accuracy metric.
body: The score is meant to shortlist candidates, not replace judgment. Two close scores should send you to the overlay and diagnostics rather than straight to promotion.

This surface does not yet compare predictions against a labeled benchmark clip. It ranks runtime evidence from this app itself, which is why the score is helpful for review but not the same thing as model accuracy.

External baselines can still appear in the same benchmark run even when they do not emit the native metrics required for the 0-100 proxy score. In those cases, review the overlay, logs, and diagnostics directly instead of reading a missing score as failure.

title: Benchmark leaderboard
summary: The leaderboard ranks candidates for one benchmark run and should be read together with overlay and diagnostics evidence.
body: A candidate can score well overall but still have suspicious overlay behavior on hard moments. That is why this surface keeps the selected candidate detail beside the ranking.

Use the raw sub-metrics and the overlay video to understand why a model ranked where it did. If you need official accuracy, use a labeled SoccerNetGS evaluation path such as GS-HOTA instead of this proxy leaderboard.

Some rows can be side-by-side review entries rather than ranked proxy-score entries. That happens when an external baseline completes but does not emit the native runtime metrics this workbench uses for ranking.

title: sn-gamestate baseline
summary: The official SoccerNet Game State Reconstruction devkit is an external TrackLab-based baseline and evaluation reference.
body: sn-gamestate is the official SoccerNet baseline and evaluator stack for Game State Reconstruction. Its dataset and model weights are downloaded automatically on first run.

Its published accuracy contract is GS-HOTA on labeled SoccerNetGS clips. That is different from this workbench's current proxy clip-comparison score, which does not yet run the official labeled evaluator inline.
